refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-operator-to-actuator) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and responsive remote equipment control.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness and control responsiveness. The p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is a hard constraint for safe remote operation. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (not just network transit), includes both directions of communication, and is conditioned on agreed network assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit verification and traceability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Conduct operator acceptance testing with simulated remote scenarios. Log p95 latency metrics across multiple operational sessions. Validate that latency remains within bounds during peak system load and network stress conditions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Telemetry acquisition and transmission shall introduce minimal latency (target ≤ 50 ms sensor-to-network).
FR-NGHE-008.2: Network transport shall deliver telemetry and control packets with bounded latency (target ≤ 80 ms network transit, p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: Control command reception and actuation shall complete with minimal latency (target ≤ 20 ms network-to-actuator).
FR-NGHE-008.4: Teleoperation system shall monitor and report latency health and degradation to operator and safety systems.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency telemetry pipeline with hardware-accelerated sensor acquisition, edge buffering, and priority-queued network transmission; use dedicated network link with QoS guarantees and redundant paths.

DP-B: Deploy distributed edge computing nodes co-located with vehicle sensors and actuators; minimize sensor-to-network and network-to-actuator hops via local processing and direct actuation triggers.

DP-C: Establish dedicated, low-latency network infrastructure (private 5G or leased fiber) with latency SLA guarantees; implement end-to-end latency monitoring and adaptive control algorithms that degrade gracefully under latency excursions.
DP-D: Use commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) teleoperation middleware with proven sub-150 ms latency; configure network QoS and redundancy; validate latency in target deployment environment.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Establish dedicated, low-latency network infrastructure with latency SLA guarantees; implement end-to-end latency monitoring and adaptive control algorithms that degrade gracefully under latency excursions.

potential_sources: Network infrastructure design is decoupled from vehicle control logic (vehicle can operate over multiple network types if latency SLA is met).
Latency monitoring and adaptive control are decoupled from specific network technology (applicable to 5G, fiber, or hybrid).
Telemetry and tasking interfaces remain independent of network layer implementation.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C avoids tight coupling to specific hardware (DP-B) or middleware (DP-D), providing flexibility. It couples the FR to network SLA guarantees and monitoring/adaptation logic, which is appropriate for a safety-critical latency requirement. Coupling is acceptable and necessary.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Dedicated network infrastructure (5G or fiber) is available and can be provisioned in target deployment areas.
Assumption: Network SLA guarantees (p95 ≤ 80 ms) are achievable and contractually enforceable.
Risk: If network infrastructure is unavailable or SLA cannot be met, teleoperation will not be safe; fallback to manual operation or reduced autonomy required.
Risk: Adaptive control algorithms may introduce additional latency or unpredictable behavior if network degrades; requires rigorous validation.
Risk: Monitoring and reporting of latency health adds computational overhead; must not itself introduce latency.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-C is sound but should be paired with explicit fallback strategy: if latency exceeds safe threshold, system shall automatically transition to reduced-autonomy mode or manual operation. This should be reflected in sub-FR-NGHE-008.4 (latency health reporting and response).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns end-to-end teleoperation latency performance, including telemetry acquisition and transmission, control command reception and actuation, latency monitoring, and adaptive control logic. Responsible for meeting p95 ≤ 150 ms latency SLA. Interfaces with Vehicle (for sensor/actuator access), Safety Services (for latency health alerts), and Network Link (for transport).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module outputs sensor telemetry (video, position, orientation, load, diagnostics) to operator console and safety systems. Latency from sensor acquisition to telemetry output must be ≤ 50 ms.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator control commands (movement, tool actuation, mode changes) from remote operator console. Latency from tasking input to vehicle actuation must be ≤ 20 ms.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module reports latency health status and degradation alerts to Safety Services. Triggers safe shutdown or mode transition if latency exceeds safe thresholds.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module uses Network Link for bidirectional transport of telemetry and tasking packets. Assumes Network Link provides p95 ≤ 80 ms latency SLA.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Teleoperation is a major subsystem but not the entire solution. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility and obscure latency ownership. Not recommended.

Vehicle owns sensor and actuator hardware but not the teleoperation communication pipeline or network transport. Latency monitoring and adaptive control are not vehicle-specific. Assigning to Vehicle would couple teleoperation logic to vehicle hardware, reducing reusability. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module is purpose-built for remote operation. It owns telemetry acquisition, transmission, control reception, actuation, latency monitoring, and adaptive control. All sub-functions of DP-C map directly to Teleoperation scope. Excellent containment, minimal diffusion. Strongly recommended.

Safety Services owns safety monitoring and alerts but not the teleoperation pipeline itself. Latency health reporting is an output to Safety Services, not a function of Safety Services. Assigning to Safety Services would invert responsibility. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the natural and optimal owner of CR-NGHE-008. It encapsulates all latency-critical functions: telemetry pipeline, network transport coordination, control reception, and adaptive latency management. Interfaces with Vehicle (sensor/actuator access), Safety Services (health alerts), and Network Link (transport) are clean and well-defined. Containment score 0.95 reflects high alignment.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module depends on Vehicle module for sensor/actuator access. If Vehicle introduces latency (e.g., slow sensor polling), Teleoperation cannot meet SLA. Mitigation: Define strict latency budgets for Vehicle sensor/actuator interfaces (FR-NGHE-008.1 and FR-NGHE-008.3).

Risk: Teleoperation module depends on Network Link for transport SLA. If Network Link cannot guarantee p95 ≤ 80 ms, Teleoperation cannot meet overall SLA. Mitigation: Establish contractual SLA with network provider; implement fallback to reduced-autonomy mode if SLA is violated.

Risk: Adaptive control algorithms in Teleoperation may introduce unpredictable latency or instability. Mitigation: Rigorous validation and testing of adaptive algorithms under network stress conditions.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Teleoperation module assignment is sound. Recommend explicit definition of latency budgets for sub-components (sensor acquisition ≤ 50 ms, network transit ≤ 80 ms, actuation ≤ 20 ms) and clear interface contracts with Vehicle and Network Link modules. Establish monitoring and fallback mechanisms for latency SLA violations.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (dedicated low-latency network infrastructure with SLA guarantees and adaptive control) directly addresses FR-NGHE-008 (teleoperation latency ≤ 150 ms p95). The DP is specific, measurable, and achievable. Coupling analysis confirms acceptable decoupling from vehicle hardware and middleware specifics. Assumptions and risks are identified and manageable. FR-DP mapping is sound.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module must coordinate with Vehicle module for sensor/actuator latency. Ensure Vehicle module has explicit latency budgets and monitoring.
Teleoperation module must coordinate with Network Link module for transport SLA. Ensure Network Link has contractual guarantees and monitoring.
Teleoperation module must report latency health to Safety Services. Ensure SafetyAlert interface is well-defined and non-blocking.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-C. Containment score 0.95 reflects excellent alignment. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are clean and well-defined. No significant diffusion risks identified. DP-IM mapping is sound.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-008 is feasible via end-to-end latency measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can instrument telemetry pipeline, network transport, and actuation to measure and report latency metrics. Operator acceptance testing can validate safe remote operation. Verification is straightforward and achievable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-008 to Teleoperation module is clear. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) are allocated to specific sub-components of Teleoperation (sensor acquisition, network transport, actuation, monitoring). Allocation is traceable and verifiable.

recommended_revisions: Refine sub-FR-NGHE-008.4 to explicitly include fallback/degradation strategy: 'Teleoperation system shall monitor latency health and, if latency exceeds safe threshold, automatically transition to reduced-autonomy mode or manual operation, with operator notification.'

Define explicit latency budgets for Vehicle module (sensor acquisition ≤ 50 ms, actuation ≤ 20 ms) and Network Link module (transport ≤ 80 ms p95). These should be captured as separate FRs or interface contracts.
Establish monitoring and alerting mechanisms for latency SLA violations. Define thresholds, alert types, and operator response procedures.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and has been refined into four sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4). Each sub-FR addresses a distinct latency-critical function: sensor acquisition, network transport, actuation, and monitoring. DP-C and Teleoperation module assignment are sound at the current level, but further decomposition is necessary to allocate latency budgets, define interface contracts, and design specific sub-components (e.g., telemetry pipeline, adaptive control algorithms, monitoring logic). Recommend proceeding to next decomposition level for each sub-FR.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Telemetry acquisition and transmission shall introduce minimal latency (target ≤ 50 ms sensor-to-network). [Decompose into sensor polling, buffering, serialization, and transmission sub-functions.]

FR-NGHE-008.2: Network transport shall deliver telemetry and control packets with bounded latency (target ≤ 80 ms network transit, p95). [Decompose into QoS configuration, redundancy, and transport optimization sub-functions.]

FR-NGHE-008.3: Control command reception and actuation shall complete with minimal latency (target ≤ 20 ms network-to-actuator). [Decompose into command parsing, validation, and actuation trigger sub-functions.]

FR-NGHE-008.4: Teleoperation system shall monitor and report latency health and degradation to operator and safety systems. [Decompose into latency measurement, threshold detection, alerting, and fallback logic sub-functions.]

assumptions: Dedicated network infrastructure (5G or fiber) is available and can be provisioned in target deployment areas.
Network SLA guarantees (p95 ≤ 80 ms) are achievable and contractually enforceable.
Vehicle module can provide sensor data and accept actuation commands with latencies ≤ 50 ms and ≤ 20 ms respectively.
Operator console and remote control systems can transmit tasking commands and receive telemetry with acceptable latencies.
Adaptive control algorithms can be designed and validated to degrade gracefully under network latency excursions without introducing instability.
open_risks: Network infrastructure availability and SLA enforceability in target deployment areas.
Vehicle module latency performance (sensor acquisition and actuation).
Adaptive control algorithm stability and predictability under network stress.
Operator acceptance and training for reduced-autonomy fallback mode.
Integration and testing complexity of end-to-end latency measurement and monitoring.
evidence_needed: Network infrastructure feasibility study and SLA contract terms.
Vehicle module latency characterization (sensor polling, actuation response times).
Adaptive control algorithm design and validation under simulated network conditions.
Operator acceptance testing results for teleoperation latency and fallback modes.
End-to-end latency measurement data from field trials under agreed network conditions.
Safety analysis and certification evidence for latency-dependent safe operation.
